name: Alachua County Quarries
short_description: Active and inactive quarries near Newberry offering opportunities to collect quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens from Florida's phosphate-rich geology. Flat terrain with easy access.
description:
Alachua County contains several quarry sites that reflect the region's rich phosphate and clay mining heritage. The quarries expose cross-sections of the Hawthorn Group and underlying limestone formations that have been worked for over a century. Visitors can examine quartz veins, feldspar crystals, and mica flakes in the exposed rock faces and tailings piles. The flat terrain and relatively accessible locations make this a good site for collectors of all experience levels seeking Florida minerals. Always verify current access status before visiting, as quarry operations and private ownership can change.

The geology here is dominated by Miocene-aged phosphate-bearing sediments and the older Paleocene-Eocene limestone basement. These formations created ideal conditions for the silicate minerals found throughout the region. Quartz and feldspar occur as both discrete crystals and in aggregates within the host rocks. Mica sheets and flakes are common in the clay-rich layers exposed in active and reclaimed quarry faces.

This location represents a unique opportunity to study Florida's industrial mineral resources firsthand. The open pits and spoil heaps reveal layers that would otherwise remain hidden underground. Rockhounders should bring bags for specimens and be prepared for sunny, often wet conditions typical of central Florida.
history: Alachua County's quarry sites developed from extensive phosphate mining operations that began in the late 1800s and continued through the 20th century. The Hawthorn Group, a Miocene marine sediment containing phosphate nodules and silicate minerals, attracted commercial miners who extracted valuable phosphate for fertilizer. This mining activity exposed deep geological layers and created the quarries visited by rockhounders today, leaving behind excellent venues for studying Florida's subsurface mineral diversity.
tips: Search the walls and floor of inactive quarries for quartz veins that often outcrop in weathered limestone - look for white, translucent crystals. Bring a 10x hand lens to identify feldspar crystals, which appear as white or pink rectangular forms within the parent rock. Collect mica flakes from clay-rich layers and tailings piles, especially after recent rainfall when exposure is freshest. Always ask permission at active quarries and check for posted collection rules before gathering specimens.
terrain_notes: The quarry sites sit at approximately 48 feet elevation in the heart of Florida's flat topography. Terrain is completely flat with minimal slope. Ground may be muddy or waterlogged depending on recent rainfall. Vegetation includes palmettos, scrub oak, and mixed hardwoods typical of central Florida. Walking distances from parking to quarry faces vary from 100 to 500 feet depending on which site is accessed. Footing is generally firm but can be slippery near water-filled quarry pits.
best_season: October - April. Florida's summer heat and frequent afternoon thunderstorms make May through September uncomfortable and hazardous for rockhounding. Fall through spring offers lower humidity, more stable weather, and better visibility for spotting specimens.
written_directions: From Newberry, head north on County Road 234 approximately 3 miles. Look for quarry entrances and signs on the east side of the road. Some quarries are accessible from County Road 325 running east-west through the region. GPS coordinates provided should guide you to the primary quarry cluster. Confirm access by checking with local property managers or posted signage before entering any site.
road_conditions: Paved county roads lead to quarry areas. Some access roads to quarry sites may be unpaved or rough. High-clearance vehicles recommended after rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas near quarry entrances. No designated lot. Space is limited at popular sites. Arrive early during peak season (October-April).
collection_rules: Personal use collection only. Verify access with property owners before collecting. Do not enter active mining operations. Respect posted 'No Trespassing' signs. Do not remove material from marked spoil boundaries. Keep collections to hand-carried quantities. Some quarries may prohibit collection - always ask first.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Verify property access before entering
Do not enter active mining operations
Respect all 'No Trespassing' signs
Keep to hand-carried quantities
Check with site management for current collection policies
nearest_services: Newberry, 3 miles - gas, food, water, supplies available
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain accessible to most ability levels. Unpaved access roads and muddy areas may be difficult for wheelchairs or walkers. Sturdy footwear recommended due to sharp rocks and uneven ground near quarry edges. Stay clear of steep pit edges and deep water-filled sections.

faq.question: What gems and minerals can I find at Alachua County Quarries near Newberry?
faq.answer: The primary specimens found here are quartz crystals, feldspar (white and pink varieties), and mica flakes. These minerals occur in the exposed Miocene-age phosphate-bearing rocks and underlying limestone formations that are exposed in the quarry faces and spoil piles. Specimens range from small crystals to larger aggregates.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Alachua County Quarries free or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Collection at these quarries is free with no permit required, but access depends entirely on property ownership and current use status. Some sites remain active mining operations while others are reclaimed. You must verify permission before collecting and respect all posted signs and boundaries.

faq.question: When is the best time to go rockhounding at Alachua County Quarries?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Florida's summer heat, humidity, and frequent afternoon thunderstorms make those months unpleasant and potentially dangerous. Fall and spring offer cooler temperatures, lower moisture, and better overall collecting conditions.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at these quarries?
faq.answer: Bring a 10x hand lens for identifying feldspar and mica, a small pick or hammer for extracting specimens, collection bags, sturdy boots with good grip, sun protection, and plenty of water. A backpack makes transporting specimens easier. Gloves protect against sharp rock edges.

faq.question: Are Alachua County Quarries beginner-friendly for new rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, these quarries are excellent for beginners. The flat terrain requires no climbing, specimens are relatively easy to spot and extract, and the area is accessible year-round during the appropriate season. No advanced skills or special equipment are needed, making this ideal for families and first-time collectors.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog rockhounding at Alachua County Quarries?
faq.answer: Dogs are generally welcome at these public quarry areas, but always verify current access policies before visiting. Keep dogs on leash near steep quarry edges and deep water-filled pits for safety. Bring extra water for pets during warmer months, and watch for fire ants and snakes in tall grass.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Alachua County Quarries near Newberry, Florida. Free public access, easy flat terrain, beginner-friendly. Collect year-round.
